file_name: Ningrum et al. - 2021 - Deep learning classifier with patient’s metadata of dermoscopic images in malignant melanoma detecti.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning models, specifically Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and Artificial Neural Networks (ANN), to develop a classifier for malignant melanoma detection using dermoscopic images and patient metadata. The CNN+ANN model achieved a balanced accuracy of 92.34% in binary classification of melanoma vs non-melanoma.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled 'ORIGINAL RESEARCH' in the header. It presents original experimental work involving the development and validation of a deep learning classifier (CNN+ANN) using the ISIC Archive dataset to detect malignant melanoma, including specific methods, results, and performance metrics.

title: Deep Learning Classifier with Patient’s Metadata of Dermoscopic Images in Malignant Melanoma Detection
brief_summary: This study developed a deep learning classifier for malignant melanoma detection using dermoscopic images and patient metadata to address challenges in low-resource settings. The researchers utilized the ISIC Archive dataset, selecting 1,200 biopsy-proven dermoscopic images and corresponding metadata (age, gender, anatomic site) for binary classification of melanoma versus non-melanoma. Two models were compared: a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) using only images and a combined CNN+Artificial Neural Network (ANN) model integrating image data with patient metadata. The CNN+ANN model achieved a balanced accuracy of 92.34%, significantly outperforming the CNN-only model's 73.69% accuracy. The combination of metadata prevented overfitting observed in the image-only model and resulted in a small model size (24 MB) suitable for deployment on devices with limited computational resources. The authors conclude that this multimodal approach is promising for early melanoma screening in remote and low-resource healthcare environments.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for binary classification. The study used 374 malignant melanoma cases in the final dataset (300 for testing, 74 for training/validation subset) derived from the ISIC Archive/HAM10000.

The comparator class in the binary classification task. The study used 826 non-malignant cases in the final dataset (300 for testing, 526 for training/validation subset). The paper notes that the original dataset contained various lesion types including nevus, pigmented benign keratosis, basal cell carcinoma, and seborrheic keratosis, but these were grouped into the 'nonmalignant' class for this specific experiment.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of malignant melanoma vs non-malignant melanoma
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the objective is 'binary classification malignant melanomas vs nonmalignant melanomas'. The models (CNN and CNN+ANN) output class predictions for these two discrete diagnostic categories. Performance was evaluated using metrics such as accuracy, balanced accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and AUROC, which are standard for classification tasks.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Archive (ISIC 2019: Training) / HAM10000
datasets_used.description: An open-access dermatology repository from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC). The study utilized data from the 'ISIC 2019: Training' dataset, which is part of the HAM10000 collection (along with MSK-1, MSK-2, MSK-3, and MSK-4 datasets). The original dataset contained approximately 9,000 dermoscopic images with biopsy-proven diagnoses including nevus, melanoma, pigmented benign keratosis, basal cell carcinoma, and seborrheic keratosis. The study selected a subset of 1,200 images for binary classification.

use.training: Used to train the CNN and CNN+ANN models. The training set consisted of 720 images (281 malignant, 619 benign) derived from the 900 images allocated for training/validation.
use.validation: Used to validate the model during training. The validation set consisted of 180 images, split from the training pool with a 0.2 ratio.
use.testing: Used to evaluate the final performance of the models. The testing set consisted of 300 images (93 malignant, 207 nonmalignant).
use.other: The original source dataset (HAM10000/ISIC 2019 Training) contained ~9,000 images with classes: nevus, melanoma, pigmented benign keratosis, basal cell carcinoma, seborrheic keratosis, and others. These were grouped into 'Malignant' and 'Nonmalignant' for this study.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To assess the statistical significance of differences in patient metadata (age, gender, anatomy site, location) between malignant and nonmalignant melanoma groups.
To summarize the distribution of age in malignant and nonmalignant melanoma groups.
To visualize algorithm performance, identify misclassifications, and calculate sensitivity, specificity, accuracy, and balanced accuracy.

statistical_methods_used.method: Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUROC)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate and compare the overall classification performance of the CNN model versus the CNN+ANN model across all possible thresholds.
To visualize the label distributions and separation of malignant and nonmalignant melanoma predictions in a two-dimensional plot.

hypothesis_testing.test: p-value calculation (specifically for comparing patient metadata distributions between malignant and nonmalignant groups)
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no significant difference in the distribution of patient metadata (age, gender, anatomy site, location) between the malignant melanoma and nonmalignant melanoma groups.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference in the distribution of patient metadata (age, gender, anatomy site, location) between the malignant melanoma and nonmalignant melanoma groups.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper reports p-values for the comparison of patient metadata distributions. For age, the p-value was <0.01, indicating a significant difference (mean age 62.6 years for malignant vs 17.5 years for nonmalignant). For anatomy site, the p-value was 0.01, indicating a significant difference. For gender and location, p-values were 0.38 and 0.09 respectively, indicating no significant difference. The threshold for significance was implicitly 0.05 based on the reporting of <0.01 as significant.

CNN (Image only).Notes: Demonstrated overfitting due to large gap between training and validation loss/accuracy. Many misclassifications of nonmalignant melanoma.

CNN+ANN (Image + Patient Metadata).Notes: Outperformed CNN model. Combination of metadata prevented overfitting. Model size 24-25.4 MB.

ISIC Archive (ISIC 2019: Training / HAM1.notes: The CNN+ANN model achieved a balanced accuracy of 92.34%, significantly higher than the CNN model's 73.69%. The CNN+ANN model showed better separation of malignant and nonmalignant classes in t-SNE plots compared to the CNN model.

architecture_used.main_model: CNN+ANN (Convolutional Neural Network combined with Artificial Neural Network)
architecture_used.input_size: 300x300 pixels (for images); categorical variables (anatomy site, location, gender) and numerical variable (age) for metadata

architecture_details.framework: Keras application program interface with TensorFlow backend (version 1.14)
architecture_details.image_preprocessing: Cropping to remove stickers/rulers/hair; resizing to 300x300 pixels using bilinear interpolation from patches at various scales (1/5, 2/5, 3/5, 4/5 of original size); on-the-fly data augmentation (vertical/horizontal flips, random rotation ±10°, zoom 90-110%)
architecture_details.metadata_preprocessing: One-hot encoding for categorical variables (anatomy site, location, gender); MinMaxScaler for numerical variable (age)
architecture_details.cnn_structure: Convolution layers, pooling layers to reduce dimensionality, flattening of output, dropout (inputs dropped to 0 to reduce overfitting), activation functions
architecture_details.ann_structure: Concatenation of CNN model output with ANN input; fully connected layers; activation function for classification

training_details.hyperparameters: Dropout used to reduce overfitting; learning rate and batch size not explicitly reported
training_details.validation_strategy: Validation set used to monitor loss and accuracy; training stopped when validation loss no longer decreased

classification_or_output_head.type: Binary classification (Malignant Melanoma vs Nonmalignant Melanoma)

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: CNN+ANN (Convolutional Neural Network combined with Artificial Neural Network)
preprocessing_techniques: Image cropping to remove stickers, rulers, and hair artifacts
Patch extraction: Randomly selecting rectangular patches at various scales (1/5, 2/5, 3/5, and 4/5 of original size) from the middle of every picture
Image resizing: Reorganizing patches into 300x300 pixels using bilinear interpolation
On-the-fly data augmentation: Vertical and horizontal flips, random rotation (-10°, +10°), and zoom (90-110% of length and width)
Metadata preprocessing: One-hot encoding for categorical variables (anatomy site, location, gender)
Metadata preprocessing: MinMaxScaler for numerical variable (age)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied during training; included vertical and horizontal flips, random rotation (-10° to +10°), and zoom (90-110% of length and width).
Randomly selecting rectangular patches at various scales (1/5, 2/5, 3/5, and 4/5 of original size) from the middle of every picture to generate training data.

conclusions: The CNN+ANN model, which combines dermoscopic images with patient metadata (age, gender, anatomic site), achieved a balanced accuracy of 92.34%, significantly outperforming the CNN-only model's balanced accuracy of 73.69%.
The integration of patient metadata via an Artificial Neural Network (ANN) prevented the overfitting observed in the CNN model that used only image data.
The CNN+ANN model demonstrated superior classification performance with an AUROC of 97.10%, compared to 82.40% for the CNN-only model.

The final model size was small (24-25.4 MB), allowing it to run on medium-class computers without cloud computing or high-end GPUs, making it suitable for deployment on devices with limited resources.
The multimodal approach is promising for development as a screening work device in remote and low-resource healthcare settings.
The study utilized a limited and unbalanced dataset (1,200 images) to better reflect clinical settings, yet still achieved high performance comparable to previous studies using larger datasets.
important_remarks: The study utilized a limited and unbalanced dataset (1,200 images) derived from the ISIC Archive/HAM10000, where non-malignant lesions were grouped into a single class, potentially masking performance differences among specific benign lesion types.

The CNN-only model exhibited significant overfitting, evidenced by a large gap between training and validation loss/accuracy, which was mitigated by the addition of patient metadata in the CNN+ANN model.

Patient metadata showed significant distribution differences between classes (age p<0.01, anatomy site p=0.01), suggesting that the model's performance improvement may be partly driven by these demographic/anatomical biases rather than purely visual features.
Data preprocessing involved manual cropping to remove artifacts (stickers, rulers, hair), which introduces potential human bias and limits full automation for deployment.

The model was validated only on a held-out test set from the same source dataset (ISIC Archive); there is no external validation on independent clinical datasets, limiting generalizability to diverse populations.

The study highlights the model's small size (24 MB) and low computational requirements as a key strength for deployment in low-resource and remote healthcare settings, addressing a specific gap in existing deep learning solutions.
